verses.text: The Pharisees and Sadducees approached to test Him, asking for a sign from heaven.
He replied, “When evening comes, you say, ‘Fair weather, for the sky is red,’
and in the morning, ‘Stormy weather, for the sky is red and overcast.’ You know how to interpret the sky but fail to discern the signs of the times.
An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign, but none will be given except the sign of Jonah.” Then he left them and went away.
When the disciples crossed to the other side, they forgot to bring bread.
Jesus said to them, “Watch and beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
They discussed this among themselves, saying, “It is because we did not bring any bread.”
Aware of this, Jesus said, “You of little faith, why are you talking about having no bread?
Do you still not understand? Do you not remember the five loaves for the five thousand, and the full baskets you gathered?
Or the seven loaves for the four thousand, and how many baskets you gathered?
How do you not understand that I was not speaking about bread? Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
Then they understood that he was not talking about bread, but about the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.

sections.headline: The Rock Declares Jesus as the Christ

verses.text: When Jesus came to the region of Caesarea Philippi, he asked his disciples, “Who do people say the Son of Man is?”
They replied, “Some say John the Baptist, others Elijah, and still others Jeremiah or one of the prophets.”
He said to them, “But who do you say I am?”
Simon Peter answered, “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.”
Jesus replied, “Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah, for this was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by my Father in heaven.
And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it.
I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven; whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven.”
Then he commanded his disciples not to tell anyone that he was the Christ.
From that time, Jesus began to tell his disciples that he must go to Jerusalem, suffer many things from the elders, chief priests, and scribes, be killed, and be raised on the third day.
Then Peter took Him aside and began to rebuke Him, saying, “Far be it from you, Lord! This will never happen to you!”
But Jesus turned and said to Peter, “Get behind me, Satan! You are a stumbling block to me, for you are not mindful of the things of God, but the things of people.”
Then Jesus said to his disciples, “If anyone desires to come after me, they must deny themselves, take up their cross, and follow me.”
For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for my sake will find it.
For what will it profit someone to gain the whole world if they lose their soul? Or what can someone give in exchange for their soul?
For the Son of Man will come in the glory of his Father with his angels, and then he will reward each one according to their deeds.
Truly, I tell you, some standing here will not taste death until they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom.

footnotes.content: The term 'adulterous generation' refers to a spiritually unfaithful people, echoing the language of the Old Testament prophets like Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel, who described Israel's idolatry and covenant betrayal as spiritual adultery. In this context, Jesus is condemning the generation that rejects his message despite the clear evidence of his divine authority. The 'sign of Jonah' refers to Jonah's three days in the belly of the great fish (Jonah 1:17), foreshadowing Jesus' own death, burial, and resurrection. It is the only sign Jesus offers as proof to those demanding miraculous confirmation, emphasizing faith in his resurrection over spectacle.

‘Leaven’ was a common Jewish metaphor for influence or corruption. Jesus used it here to refer not to physical bread but to the doctrinal distortion and spiritual hypocrisy of the Pharisees and Sadducees.
Jesus' statement, 'You are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church,' includes a deliberate Greek wordplay between 'Petros' ('stone') and 'petra' ('rock' or 'bedrock').
The 'keys of the kingdom of heaven' represent spiritual authority to open and shut access, drawn from Jewish imagery (cf. Isaiah 22:22).
The phrase 'merely human concerns' refers to thoughts shaped by natural instinct, fear, or worldly reasoning.
The Greek word for 'life' is 'psychē,' which also means 'soul,' referring to one's eternal self.
The phrase 'forfeit their soul' uses the Greek verb 'zēmiōthēnai,' meaning to suffer loss, be penalized, or forfeit.
This verse has several interpretations, including the Transfiguration, the resurrection, Pentecost, or the 70 AD judgment on Jerusalem.
